claims.text: In 2023, villagers in the Peruvian Amazon reported being attacked by 7-foot-tall hovering humanoids with black armor and glowing yellow eyes that they called 'los pelicaras' — the face peelers.

evidence.quote:
another victim of los pelicaras,
the face peelers

claims.rationale: Host opens with detailed villager testimony from San Antonio de Pintiaku and other Amazon villages in summer 2023. Multiple witnesses, Peruvian press coverage, and Tim Alberino's documented expedition corroborate that villagers reported these attacks. What happened physically remains disputed, but that they reported this experience is well-established.

claims.text: The Peruvian government concluded the attackers were illegal gold miners using jetpacks to scare villagers off their land.

evidence.quote:
were illegal gold miners using jetpacks
to scare the villagers

claims.rationale: Host presents the official jetpack-miner explanation explicitly as the story he is about to demolish — commercial jetpacks are loud, short-endurance, and can't hover silently for hours; the nearby city of Na has no gold but had sightings anyway. Host calls it convenient and dismissive, so directTruth low.

claims.text: Commercial jetpacks are loud, hard to control, and can fly for only a few minutes — incompatible with the hours-long silent hovering villagers reported.

evidence.quote:
jetpacks are loud, hard to control, and
can fly only for a few minutes.

claims.rationale: Well-documented engineering fact. JetPack Aviation's JB-11 flies ~10 minutes; Gravity Industries jet suit ~5-9 minutes; all commercial units are audibly loud due to turbine exhaust. Aligns with public specifications.

dependencies.rationale: [debunks] If commercial jetpacks can't hover silently for hours, the illegal-miner-with-jetpacks explanation fails on its technical premise.

claims.text: The U.S. military ran a joint exercise called Resolute Sentinel 2023 out of Iquitos naval base, with 87 Marines in the region until July 9th, 2023.

evidence.quote:
Resolute
Sentinel 2023, right out of Aikito's
naval base, just up river from San
Antonio to Pintoyaku to Leah's village.
87 US Marines

claims.rationale: Resolute Sentinel is a real annual SOUTHCOM humanitarian/military exercise in Peru. The 2023 iteration ran in Loreto and other regions; U.S. Southern Command press releases confirm Marines, Air Force, and Space Force participation. Dates align with public reporting.

claims.text: The face-peeler attacks in San Antonio de Pintiaku began on July 11th, 2023 — just two days after the U.S. military training exercise ended.

evidence.quote:
ended on July 9th. The attacks began on
July 11

claims.rationale: Host stresses this as 'fact, not theory.' Resolute Sentinel 2023 end date and Pelicatus attack start dates are both reported in Peruvian press. Correlation is solid; host uses it to argue causation, so I discount slightly for the causal inference the correlation invites.

dependencies.rationale: Establishing the exercise dates is a prerequisite for the 2-day-gap correlation claim.

claims.text: Tia Davila heard her attackers speaking Spanish — one with a local Peruvian accent, the other with an American accent.

evidence.quote:
Two voices, one local, the other a
gringo, an American.

claims.rationale: Single witness testimony from a traumatized teen. Host treats it as central evidence for the American-involvement theory. Uncorroborated by other witnesses, so omitting directTruth — it's a reported claim, not independently verified.

claims.text: The Pishtaco legend — a pale-skinned man who hunts at night and peels skin from victims — originated in the 1500s when Spanish conquistadors were accused of harvesting human fat from Andean bodies.

evidence.quote:
In the 1500s, when Spanish concistadors
tore through Peru with steel and
gunpow

claims.rationale: The pishtaco is a well-documented Andean folk figure extensively covered in Latin American ethnography (Mary Weismantel's 'Cholas and Pishtacos' is the standard reference). Colonial-era origin and fat-extraction motif align with published scholarship.

claims.text: In 2009, Peruvian police arrested a gang carrying liquefied human fat in soda bottles that was allegedly sold to European cosmetics markets at $36,000 per gallon.

evidence.quote:
2009, Provian
police arrested a gang carrying plastic
soda bottles filled with liqufied human
fat. They were selling it on the
European black market. Human fat was

claims.rationale: The November 2009 Peruvian police announcement of the 'Pishtacos' gang was widely reported (BBC, Reuters). However, Peruvian journalists and police later questioned the cosmetics-market angle; the $36,000/gallon figure and European sale never had hard corroboration. The arrest is real; the human-fat-to-cosmetics pipeline is dubious.

dependencies.rationale: The 2009 arrest is offered as modern evidence that the pishtaco legend has real economic substrate.

claims.text: In the 1950s, the CIA faked vampire-style killings in the Philippines to psychologically terrorize Hukbalahap communist insurgents.

evidence.quote:
CIA once faked vampire attacks as
part of a counterinsurgency strategy. In
the 1950s in the Philippines, a
commun

claims.rationale: The Edward Lansdale aswang psy-op against the Huks is documented in Lansdale's memoir 'In the Midst of Wars' (1972) and in subsequent declassified material. Episode details (hanging drained corpses) are widely cited. A real event whose specifics have been embellished in retelling.

claims.text: The attackers' reported capabilities — silent hovering, exoskeleton armor, directed-energy weapons, night-vision helmets — match experimental U.S. military technology better than any commercial system.

evidence.quote:
's consistent with
experimental silent lift systems using
advanced ducted fans or even
electromagnetic propulsion. The armor
sounds a lot like the Talos suit, a

claims.rationale: Host's central inferential claim. TALOS (Tactical Assault Light Operator Suit) is a real but officially cancelled USSOCOM program; the Active Denial System is a real millimeter-wave directed-energy weapon. Mapping is suggestive but speculative — omitting directTruth because matching capability descriptions to armor types isn't verification.

dependencies.rationale: Having shown commercial jetpacks can't account for the witness reports, the witness reports now need an alternative tech source — experimental military kit fills that gap.
The American-accent testimony and American-adjacent military presence combine to make joint U.S. involvement the natural candidate.

claims.text: Tim Alberino's theory — that the attacks are a shadowy international organization using reverse-engineered alien technology for organ harvesting and sex trafficking — has no direct proof.

evidence.quote:
Tim Alberino's theory
about a shadowy organization harvesting
organs using alien technology? That's
probably the best theory we have, but
there's no proof to supp

claims.rationale: Host himself concedes 'no proof.' The claim stacks multiple extraordinary components — reverse-engineered alien tech, global trafficking syndicate, operational use in Peru — each of which requires evidence, none provided. Speculation, not verdict.

claims.text: The host's verdict: the face peelers are not aliens but human perpetrators — organized, technologically advanced, and profit-driven — operating behind a supernatural cover story.

evidence.quote:
iens. I'm pretty
sure this is being done by men

motive was profit, and the method
was terror

claims.rationale: Host's explicit verdict-section statement. Opinion claim synthesizing the episode's investigation. Omitting directTruth because the verdict is a judgment about which speculative theory is most plausible, not a verified fact — the entire question remains officially unresolved.

dependencies.rationale: [alternative] Host rejects the government's jetpack-miner story and proposes an alternative — organized human actors with advanced tech.
Identifying experimental U.S. military kit as the capability source is the core of the 'human perpetrators' verdict.
